title: Question 1 (Reed and Seymour)
statement_text: Does every graph have a chordal partition such that each part is bipartite?
context_text: Reed and Seymour [1998] introduced chordal partitions as a tool for studying Hadwiger's Conjecture. A positive answer would imply that every graph with no $K_{t+1}$-minor is $2t$-colourable, representing a major breakthrough. The question was repeated in subsequent surveys.
paper_contribution: Proves the answer is 'no' via Theorem 2: for every integer $k \geq 1$ there is a graph $G$ such that for every chordal partition $P$ of $G$, some part of $P$ contains $K_k$; moreover, for every integer $t \geq 4$ there is a graph $G$ with tree-width at most $t-1$ (and thus no $K_{t+1}$-minor) such that some part of every chordal partition contains a complete graph on at least $\lfloor(3t-11)^{1/3}\rfloor$ vertices.
